summary:
The Fall of Constantinople (May 29, 1453 AD) was one of the most symbolically momentous events of the medieval world, ending the Byzantine Empire after over a thousand years of continuous existence — the last remnant of the Roman Empire — and marking the definitive establishment of the [[event:94|Ottoman Empire]] as the dominant power of the eastern Mediterranean. The 21-year-old Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II besieged the city with an army of 80,000–100,000 men and, crucially, a battery of massive bronze cannon (including the famous 'Basilica' gun) that could fire stone balls weighing hundreds of kilograms — rendering the Theodosian walls, impregnable for a thousand years, vulnerable for the first time.

The Byzantine Emperor Constantine XI had perhaps 7,000 defenders — a mix of Byzantine Greeks, Genoese, Venetians, and other volunteers — to hold walls stretching 22 kilometers. The defense lasted 53 days; Constantine died fighting on the walls when the city fell. Mehmed II entered Constantinople that afternoon and prayed at the Hagia Sophia, which he converted into a mosque. He styled himself 'Kayser-i Rum' (Caesar of Rome) and made Constantinople (renamed Istanbul) the new Ottoman capital. The fall sent shock waves across Christendom — a pope called for a crusade, and scholars (including many Greek refugees) flooded into Italy carrying manuscripts that helped accelerate the [[event:96|Renaissance]]. For modern historians, the fall of Constantinople has served as a convenient boundary marker between the medieval and early modern periods. Practically, it strengthened the Ottoman control of eastern Mediterranean trade routes, increasing European motivation to find alternative sea routes — contributing to the impetus for the [[event:97|Age of Exploration]].

The last emperor, Constantine XI, had no body recovered — a fact that generated the legend of the 'Marble Emperor,' who would one day wake and drive the Turks from Constantinople, a legend that endured in Greek popular culture until the 20th century.
